---
title: "Is this normally distributed?"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: default
    embed-resources: true
---

```{r}
#| include: false
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(purrr)
library(tibble)
library(moments)
```

## Sample data from a normally distributed population

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 5
#| layout-ncol: 2

set.seed(100000)
data_simulated <- rnorm(30)

```

## Is this normally distributed?

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 6
#| fig-height: 2.5
#| layout-ncol: 1

ggplot() +
  aes(x = data_simulated) +
  geom_density() +
  theme_linedraw() +
  xlab("Score") +
  ylab("Probability density")

ggplot() +
  aes(x = data_simulated) +
  geom_histogram() +
  theme_linedraw() +
  xlab("Score") +
  ylab("Frequency")
```

## Yes it is normal!

- We sampled from a normal population. 

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| fig-width: 10

set.seed(100000)
data_simulated <- rnorm(30)

```

- We know the ground truth
  - ie the Data Generating Process
- We must get used to thinking this way with Monte Carlo simulations
